narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40 and an impressive LSAT score of 42, you've clearly worked hard to build a strong academic foundation, including ranking in the 8th decile in your first year of school and upper-middle family income that reflects stability. However, the university's admission model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 62.6% chance that you would fail the bar exam—above the threshold they use to approve applications, leading to the regrettable rejection of yours. This prediction wasn't random; it was driven primarily by your undergraduate GPA, which stood out as the biggest factor pushing the model toward expecting a bar failure, even though your 3.40 is above the typical student's 3.21 average. Next in influence was your 9th-decile ranking in your third year of school, which actually worked in your favor by suggesting stronger potential for passing, countering some of the concerns. Your family's upper-middle income level also nudged the prediction toward failure risk, as the model associates it with certain patterns seen in past data. Your first-year 8th-decile ranking added a moderate push toward the failure prediction, while your standout LSAT score provided a small positive boost toward passing, and your full-time student status had negligible impact. Compared to typical applicants, your LSAT shines brighter than the average 36.7, and your GPA is solid, but the model weighed your later school decile drop and income bracket more heavily against you in this specific combination. It's tough to hear, especially with your strengths, but this highlights how even high performers can face hurdles if certain areas like GPA signal risks to the bar. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT or bolstering your GPA through post-baccalaureate courses to shift future predictions, and target schools with holistic reviews that might value your full profile more deeply. You have the drive—many students in similar spots have rebounded stronger. Keep pushing; your path to law isn't closed, just rerouted.
